title: Area of Combined Rectangles (Decompose Composite Figures)
essential_question: How do you find the area of a shape made by combining rectangles?

pretest.question: An L-shape splits into a 4×5 rectangle and a 3×2 rectangle. Total area?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

pretest.question: What is the BEST first step to find area of an L-shape?
choices: A) Multiply the longest side by itself

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

feedback.incorrect: Split the L into 2 rectangles, find each area, then add.

pretest.question: A T-shape splits into 6×2 (top) and 2×4 (stem). Total area?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

pretest.question: Whole rectangle: 8×5=40. Removed corner: 2×3=6. Remaining area?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

pretest.question: Which strategy works for finding L-shape area?
choices: A) Split into 2 rectangles, add areas
B) Find area of full rectangle, subtract removed corner

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

feedback.incorrect: Both approaches work — split-and-add OR full-minus-removed.

learn.title: Composite Figures
learn.content: A COMPOSITE FIGURE is a shape made of two or more simple shapes (like rectangles) joined together. Examples: L-shape, T-shape, plus-shape, irregular floor plans.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Decompose into Rectangles
learn.content: To DECOMPOSE means to break apart. To find the area of a composite figure, decompose it into simple rectangles. Then find each rectangle's area.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Add the Areas
learn.content: After decomposing, ADD the areas of all rectangles. Total = Area1 + Area2 + Area3 (and so on). Make sure no parts overlap and no parts are missed.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Decompose-Subtract Strategy
learn.content: Sometimes it's easier to find the area of the full RECTANGLE around the shape, then SUBTRACT the missing piece. Example: a square with a corner cut off — find full square's area, subtract the corner.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Choose Your Strategy
learn.content: (1) DECOMPOSE-ADD: split into rectangles, add areas. Best when shape splits cleanly. (2) FULL-MINUS-MISSING: find big rectangle, subtract missing corner. Best when only one corner is missing.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Many Ways to Decompose
learn.content: There can be MANY ways to decompose the same shape. An L can be split into 2 rectangles in different ways. As long as no overlap and no missed parts, the total area is the same.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Find Missing Dimensions
learn.content: Sometimes a composite figure problem leaves out a dimension. Use the dimensions you have: opposite sides of a big rectangle should match. Add or subtract to find missing parts.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Lesson Summary
learn.content: Today: (1) Composite figures = made of multiple rectangles. (2) Decompose-add: split + sum. (3) Full-minus-missing: big rectangle - missing piece. (4) Multiple decompositions give the same area. (5) Use given dimensions to find missing ones.

try.question: Plus-shape: 1×5 horizontal + 5×1 vertical (overlap is the center 1×1). Decompose: top arm 1×2, middle 5×1, bottom arm 1×2. Total?
solution_steps: Top arm: 1×2=2. Middle row: 5×1=5. Bottom arm: 1×2=2. Total: 9.

try.question: Which strategy is BEST when only a small corner is missing from a rectangle?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L5_combined_rectangles
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Easier to subtract small missing piece.

hints: Subtracting one small piece is easier.

feedback.incorrect: When small corner missing → full minus missing is fastest.

practice_r3.question: A pool is built in an L-shape: 8 m × 4 m main + 3 m × 2 m extension. The deck adds 1 m on every outside edge. Calculate the area of just the L-shaped pool (not deck) in sq m.

feedback.incorrect: Pool only: 32+6=38 sq m. Deck doesn't count.
